ArchitectureNotes.objective: Deliver a minimal but verifiable SOCKS5-over-secure-tunnel proxy path where local proxy-aware applications route traffic through a transport-agnostic stream abstraction backed by WSS primary transport and iroh fallback transport.
ArchitectureNotes.non-goal: Do not attempt a full VPN, browser control plane, or multi-tenant public relay in the first execution waves.
ArchitectureNotes.risk-1: Transport-specific behavior must stay behind a thin shared stream and adapter contract or ProxyBridge will become tightly coupled to WSS and iroh internals.
ArchitectureNotes.risk-2: Shutdown, fallback, backpressure, burst observability, exact reply mapping, and fast-fail SOCKS5 semantics must be first-class architectural concerns rather than postponed hardening tasks.

M-CLI.NAME: CliApp

contract.purpose: Select runtime mode, load configuration, initialize observability, launch the selected runtime surface, and coordinate graceful shutdown sequencing.

export-run.PURPOSE: Bootstrap the process and prepare startup artifacts for the selected runtime mode.

export-runUntilShutdown.PURPOSE: Launch the selected runtime surface and keep it alive until cancellation.

export-coordinateShutdown.PURPOSE: Drive accept-stop, drain, and transport-release shutdown phases.

M-CLI.depends: M-CONFIG, M-OBS, M-AUTH, M-TLS, M-WSS-GATEWAY, M-SOCKS5, M-PROXY-BRIDGE, M-SESSION

notes.note-1: CLI orchestrates startup, live runtime launch, and shutdown while transport internals stay inside the gateway, bridge, and session modules.

contract.purpose: Expose a deployable n0wss executable that forwards process arguments into the governed CLI runtime surface and returns stable exit codes.

export-main.PURPOSE: Forward argv into the library CLI runtime entrypoint and map bootstrap or runtime errors to a shell-visible exit code.

notes.note-1: This wrapper exists so release builds emit a deployable executable without duplicating bootstrap logic from M-CLI.

contract.purpose: Load and validate runtime configuration for client and server modes including bind addresses, TLS paths, auth material, transport policy, queue or session limits, timeout settings, retry policy, and burst detection thresholds.

export-loadConfig.PURPOSE: Read and validate configuration into a typed structure.

notes.note-1: Limits, timeout policy, retry settings, and burst thresholds belong in config from day one because they shape runtime behavior and tests.

contract.purpose: Initialize tracing, stable field propagation, metrics collection, sliding-window burst detection, peak-rate gauges, and redaction-aware logging behavior.

export-initObservability.PURPOSE: Configure structured logging, counters, gauges, histograms, burst detection, and subscriber installation.

export-recordBurst.PURPOSE: Record queue-rejection events into a sliding-window detector and aggregated logs.

export-resetPeak.PURPOSE: Reset peak-rate gauges on the configured maintenance interval.

notes.note-1: Redaction policy and burst observability must be enforced consistently before any auth or transport failure is logged.

contract.purpose: Validate tunnel admission policy for static tokens or tickets and redact sensitive values before logs or errors are emitted.

export-validateHandshake.PURPOSE: Authorize or reject a tunnel handshake.

export-redactSecret.PURPOSE: Normalize sensitive values before they reach logs or user-facing errors.

notes.note-1: Gateway calls auth but never handles raw tokens beyond boundary parsing.

contract.purpose: Load TLS material and expose accept/connect operations used by the websocket transport boundary.

export-fromConfig.PURPOSE: Build a deterministic TLS context from configured certificate material.

export-accept.PURPOSE: Wrap a server-side TCP stream with TLS.

export-connect.PURPOSE: Wrap a client-side TCP stream with TLS and SNI.

notes.note-1: TLS handling stays isolated from websocket session logic to keep failures deterministic and auditable.

contract.purpose: Create WSS-backed transport streams by composing TCP, TLS, websocket upgrade, and auth validation under the shared adapter contract without owning transport selection logic.

export-runServer.PURPOSE: Run the remote WSS listener and create accepted transport streams.

export-openStream.PURPOSE: Open an outbound WSS transport stream for a resolved target.

export-taskTracker.PURPOSE: Expose adapter-scoped task tracking for tests and shutdown coordination.

export-stopAccept.PURPOSE: Stop accepting new WSS connections during shutdown.

notes.note-1: Gateway is glue only: TCP accept, TLS, WS upgrade, auth invocation, and stream wrapping.

contract.purpose: Open iroh direct or relay-backed transport streams under the shared adapter contract and clean up partial state on cancel or error.

export-openStream.PURPOSE: Try to create an iroh direct or relay transport stream for a target peer.

export-taskTracker.PURPOSE: Expose adapter-scoped task tracking for tests and shutdown coordination.

export-release.PURPOSE: Release iroh transport resources during session close or shutdown.

notes.note-1: Iroh stays interchangeable only if its direct and relay details remain behind the adapter and stream abstractions.

contract.purpose: Act as a thin orchestrator over session registry, transport selection, pure per-session state transitions, and a top-level typed effect dispatcher.

export-registerSession.PURPOSE: Create a session through SessionRegistry after acquiring capacity.

export-resolveStream.PURPOSE: Delegate transport opening to TransportSelector with sequential fallback.

export-handleEvent.PURPOSE: Run StateMachine.transition and dispatch the returned top-level typed effects through EffectHandler.

export-shutdown.PURPOSE: Drain registry and close active sessions during shutdown.

notes.note-1: Internal structure is fixed as governed helper files under src/session/* and tracked as first-class graph nodes while remaining covered by the Session execution and verification surfaces.
notes.note-2: State machine must be a pure transition function with explicit SessionEffect values and no async or IO.
notes.note-3: SessionEffect top-level variants map to target components only; new subcommands must not change EffectHandler branch count.
notes.note-4: RegistryCommand and TimerCommand are local enum-hygiene concerns only; enforce small variant counts with module-local tests instead of changing architectural boundaries.

contract.purpose: Route top-level SessionEffect values to exactly one target component without embedding business logic.

export-apply.PURPOSE: Route one SessionEffect to its owning target.

export-applyAll.PURPOSE: Preserve effect order across a batch.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under M-SESSION and inherits Session verification rather than creating a separate execution wave.

contract.purpose: Represent top-level typed session effects and component-scoped command enums consumed by the session orchestrator.

export-sessionEffect.PURPOSE: Represent one top-level typed effect routed by target component.

export-registryCommand.PURPOSE: Represent registry-specific commands emitted by pure transitions.

export-timerCommand.PURPOSE: Represent timer-specific commands emitted by pure transitions.

export-metricEvent.PURPOSE: Represent observability events emitted by pure transitions.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under M-SESSION and inherits Session verification rather than creating a separate execution wave.

contract.purpose: Store session handles, enforce max-session capacity, and execute registry-targeted session effects.

export-tryReserve.PURPOSE: Reserve one session slot without waiting.

export-insert.PURPOSE: Register one session handle and consume the reservation.

export-remove.PURPOSE: Deregister a session and release capacity deterministically.

export-drainAll.PURPOSE: Clear all tracked sessions and release capacity deterministically.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under M-SESSION and inherits Session verification rather than creating a separate execution wave.

contract.purpose: Provide the pure per-session state machine used by the higher session orchestration layers.

export-transition.PURPOSE: Apply one pure transition and emit ordered typed effects.

export-sessionState.PURPOSE: Represent Active, Closing, and Closed session lifecycle states.

export-sessionEvent.PURPOSE: Represent the pure transition input surface.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under M-SESSION and inherits Session verification rather than creating a separate execution wave.

contract.purpose: Choose iroh first and fall back to WSS sequentially under bounded timeouts and explicit cancellation.

export-openStream.PURPOSE: Resolve one transport stream with explicit iroh then WSS fallback behavior.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under M-SESSION and inherits Session verification rather than creating a separate execution wave.

contract.purpose: Expose a local SOCKS5 endpoint, parse handshake and CONNECT requests, emit normalized ProxyIntent items into a bounded work queue, and return accurate client-visible reply codes on pre-pump failures.

export-runListener.PURPOSE: Bind and accept local SOCKS5 connections.

export-parseRequest.PURPOSE: Parse a SOCKS5 request into a normalized ProxyIntent.

export-mapReply.PURPOSE: Map ProxyError to an exact SOCKS5 reply code or no-reply outcome for post-pump failures without wildcard match arms.

export-stopAccept.PURPOSE: Stop accepting new local proxy connections during shutdown.

notes.note-1: Socks5Proxy knows only SOCKS5 and ProxyIntent; it does not know WSS, iroh, or session fallback policy.
notes.note-2: Bounded queue failures use try_send and must return a client-visible SOCKS5 reply immediately instead of blocking.
notes.note-3: Reply mapping must distinguish infrastructure failures, policy failures, target failures, and post-reply failures through exhaustive enum matching.
notes.note-4: CI must block any allow-list override that weakens exhaustive enum-match enforcement for reply mapping.

contract.purpose: Consume ProxyIntent items, request transport-agnostic resolved streams from SessionManager, and relay bytes bidirectionally with graceful drain behavior.

export-runWorker.PURPOSE: Consume bounded ProxyIntent queue items and process them.

export-pumpBidirectional.PURPOSE: Relay bytes between local proxy sockets and a resolved transport stream.

export-drainAll.PURPOSE: Drain active bridge workers during shutdown.

notes.note-1: ProxyBridge must never import WssGateway or IrohAdapter directly.
notes.note-2: The shared transport contracts live in governed helper files under src/transport/* and are tracked as first-class graph nodes while remaining covered by the transport-boundary and bridge verification surfaces.
notes.note-3: Pre-pump failures may produce a SOCKS5 reply; post-pump failures close the TCP path and do not remap reply codes.
notes.note-4: Both timeout and post-reply failure paths must still close local and transport write directions and notify SessionManager.

contract.purpose: Define the shared adapter contract and typed transport request used by WSS and iroh transport implementations.

export-transportRequest.PURPOSE: Carry peer label plus outbound target host and port for adapter resolution.

export-transportAdapter.PURPOSE: Expose the shared open_stream contract for transport implementations.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under the transport-boundary modules and inherits their verification surfaces.

contract.purpose: Define the transport-agnostic stream contract and resolved stream metadata shared by adapters and ProxyBridge.

export-transportStream.PURPOSE: Expose the minimal transport-agnostic stream trait used by adapter outputs.

export-resolvedStream.PURPOSE: Carry one transport stream plus transport kind metadata.

export-transportKind.PURPOSE: Discriminate Wss, IrohDirect, and IrohRelay for diagnostics.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under bridge and transport-boundary modules and inherits their verification surfaces.

contract.purpose: Provide adapter-scoped task tracking for cleanup-sensitive tests and shutdown coordination.

export-adapterTaskTracker.PURPOSE: Wrap task tracking with adapter-scoped diagnostics.

export-closeAndWait.PURPOSE: Wait for tracked tasks to finish under a bounded timeout.

notes.note-1: This helper stays execution-scoped under the transport-boundary modules and inherits their verification surfaces.

contract.purpose: Aggregate the governed transport helper modules behind one stable Rust module surface without adding transport logic.

export-adapterContract.PURPOSE: Expose the shared adapter contract submodule.

export-stream.PURPOSE: Expose the transport stream contract submodule.

export-taskTracker.PURPOSE: Expose the adapter task tracker submodule.

notes.note-1: This wrapper is now governed explicitly, but it must remain logic-free and only aggregate the transport helper modules.

contract.purpose: Expose the governed crate-root export surface and keep top-level module registration synchronized with the implementation.

export-reexportModules.PURPOSE: Expose the current governed modules through the crate root without adding runtime logic.

notes.note-1: This governed module is the crate root itself, not a leftover markup-cleanup placeholder.

contract.purpose: Prepare GitHub-facing release documentation, quickstart, changelog, and operator-facing notes that match the current implementation.

export-writeReadme.PURPOSE: Describe project overview, quickstart, and test entry points.

export-writeChangelog.PURPOSE: Capture the current release scope for GitHub release notes.

notes.note-1: Release docs must stay source-first and reflect the actual CLI and workflow commands in the repository.

contract.purpose: Define the GitHub CI and release automation surface used to validate and publish the first release candidate.

export-verifyCiWorkflow.PURPOSE: Keep GitHub CI aligned with local required checks.

export-defineReleaseFlow.PURPOSE: Describe tag and release-note expectations for GitHub publication.

notes.note-1: The first release may stay source-first if binary artifact packaging is not yet stabilized.

contract.purpose: Add a narrow smoke verification path for startup, one proxied request, and graceful shutdown before external testing begins.

export-runSmoke.PURPOSE: Exercise startup, one proxy request, and shutdown over the implemented tunnel path.

notes.note-1: Smoke coverage must stay deterministic and narrow enough to avoid flaky release gates.

contract.purpose: Fix the two-host live-wave inventory including host roles, ports, file paths, and operator-only access assumptions.

export-freezeHostRoles.PURPOSE: Declare one server host and one client host for the first live wave.

export-recordPaths.PURPOSE: Pin binary, TLS, log, and socket paths for both hosts.

notes.note-1: Credentials and passwords remain outside repository artifacts and are consumed only through operator-controlled channels.
notes.note-2: Verified live host roles: server host 91.99.128.146 reports hostname ghost-srv with bash, ss, and openssl available; client host 178.104.104.208 reports hostname ghost-cli with bash, ss, and curl available.

contract.purpose: Define the release binary, config payload, TLS assets, and staging paths used for the first live deployment.

export-pinReleaseBinary.PURPOSE: Freeze the exact release artifact or checksum deployed to both hosts.

export-defineStagingLayout.PURPOSE: Define config, cert, key, and log directory layout on each host.

notes.note-1: The same binary identity must be deployed to both hosts to keep failure analysis coherent.
notes.note-2: Release artifact target/release/n0wss was staged to /opt/n0wss/n0wss on both live hosts with matching sha256 12fdeb6fd94b2835d8705936e1fe728edfb471d13faae837af5de506d5158ec2.
notes.note-3: The first live wave uses CLI flags as runtime config payload; /opt/n0wss/certs is pre-created on both hosts and log capture remains under /var/log.

contract.purpose: Define the minimal real TLS trust path for the first live WSS server and its client trust configuration.

export-pinServerTlsPaths.PURPOSE: Freeze the cert and key paths on the server host.

export-pinClientTrust.PURPOSE: Freeze the trust material and endpoint identity expected by the client host.

notes.note-1: The first live wave assumes direct TLS termination in n0wss rather than behind a reverse proxy.
notes.note-2: Server host 91.99.128.146 now carries /opt/n0wss/certs/server.pem and /opt/n0wss/certs/server.key with a self-signed certificate valid from 2026-03-27 to 2026-04-26.
notes.note-3: Client host 178.104.104.208 trusts the copied /opt/n0wss/certs/server.pem artifact with matching sha256 3ec0bd42367df7496c1361677914654e460defe8096dd5ffc7eb4ce50635f903.
notes.note-4: The current certificate exposes SAN entries for IP 91.99.128.146 and DNS ghost-srv so the first live wave can target the real server host directly.

M-LIVE-SERVER-LAUNCH.NAME: LiveServerLaunch

contract.purpose: Define the live server-mode launch procedure, readiness evidence, and shutdown surface on the remote WSS host.

export-startServer.PURPOSE: Launch n0wss in server mode on the remote host.

export-captureServerReadiness.PURPOSE: Collect startup logs, socket state, and process identity for readiness proof.

M-LIVE-SERVER-LAUNCH.depends: M-CLI, M-CONFIG, M-AUTH, M-TLS, M-WSS-GATEWAY, M-OBS, M-SESSION

notes.note-1: Readiness evidence must include both startup anchors and bound-listener proof from the host.
notes.note-2: Live server startup on 91.99.128.146 emits Config, TLS, CliApp startup, and CliApp runtime anchors before entering the listener loop.
notes.note-3: Socket proof confirmed a bound listener on 0.0.0.0:7443 owned by n0wss during live readiness probing.

M-LIVE-CLIENT-LAUNCH.NAME: LiveClientLaunch

contract.purpose: Define the live client-mode launch procedure, local SOCKS5 readiness, and remote endpoint targeting on the second host.

export-startClient.PURPOSE: Launch n0wss in client mode on the remote client host.

export-captureClientReadiness.PURPOSE: Collect startup logs, SOCKS5 socket state, and process identity for readiness proof.

M-LIVE-CLIENT-LAUNCH.depends: M-CLI, M-CONFIG, M-SOCKS5, M-PROXY-BRIDGE, M-SESSION, M-OBS

notes.note-1: The client host must expose a local SOCKS5 endpoint and target the live WSS server URL with the pinned trust shape.
notes.note-2: Live client startup on 178.104.104.208 emits Observability, client TLS trust, CliApp startup, and CliApp runtime anchors before entering the SOCKS5 listener loop.
notes.note-3: Socket proof confirmed a bound listener on 127.0.0.1:1080 owned by n0wss during live client readiness probing.

contract.purpose: Define the first live end-to-end smoke path with one deterministic SOCKS5 request through the real two-host tunnel.

export-runLiveSmoke.PURPOSE: Execute one deterministic SOCKS5-aware request through the live tunnel.

export-captureTrajectory.PURPOSE: Collect the minimum trajectory evidence across startup, selection, reply mapping, and pump.

notes.note-1: The first live target should stay narrow and deterministic enough to separate internet reachability issues from tunnel defects.
notes.note-2: On 2026-03-27 the client host 178.104.104.208 returned Example Domain through socks5h://127.0.0.1:1080 with output size 528 bytes and exit code 0.
notes.note-3: The successful live trajectory included BLOCK_SELECT_TRANSPORT on the client host, BLOCK_PUMP_BIDIRECTIONAL on the client host, and accepted WSS plus auth evidence on the server host.

contract.purpose: Define the high-signal logs, trace anchors, socket listings, and output artifacts that make the live wave debuggable by later agents.

export-captureLogTails.PURPOSE: Capture bounded log tails from both hosts.

export-captureSocketState.PURPOSE: Capture listener and process state from both hosts.

notes.note-1: Evidence must remain small enough for a follow-up failure packet and must never include raw secrets.
notes.note-2: The first live evidence packet preserved bounded tails for /var/log/n0wss-server.log, /var/log/n0wss-client.log, and /var/log/n0wss-client-bad-auth.log plus socket-state probes and smoke output metadata.
notes.note-3: The evidence remained redacted: the reject path kept the invalid credential masked as in***en and no repository artifact stored the SSH password or raw auth token.

contract.purpose: Define the failure-packet workflow for TLS, auth, transport, proxy reply, and shutdown failures during the first live wave.

export-buildFailurePacket.PURPOSE: Condense live evidence into the canonical GRACE failure packet format.

export-routeNextAction.PURPOSE: Map the first divergent block to the next fix or rerun action.

notes.note-1: Every live failure must identify the first divergent block before another rerun is approved.
notes.note-2: The first live wave used failure packets to isolate the missing binary target, missing client TLS bootstrap, missing long-lived runtime launch, missing process-wide tracing propagation, and WSS echo semantics before the final green run.
notes.note-3: The final green run required no new failure packet because success-path evidence, bad-auth rejection evidence, and controlled shutdown evidence all satisfied Gate-Live-1.

contract.purpose: Define the canonical Linux deployment layout for binaries, certs, secret files, logs, and runtime state on managed hosts.

export-defineFilesystemLayout.PURPOSE: Pin canonical binary, config, cert, log, and runtime-state paths for each host role.

export-defineOwnershipModel.PURPOSE: Pin required file owners, modes, and write boundaries for managed deployment.

notes.note-1: Phase-9 targets /opt/n0wss for runtime assets, /etc/systemd/system for services, and bounded logs under /var/log.
notes.note-2: docs/OPERATORS.md now pins one canonical layout for shared paths, server-role paths, client-role paths, and ownership or mode rules.

contract.purpose: Define the operator secret-handling model so runtime auth material and host credentials stay out of repository artifacts and reusable evidence packets.

export-defineSecretInputs.PURPOSE: Pin which inputs come from operator-managed files, env vars, or secure out-of-band channels.

export-defineRotationFollowUp.PURPOSE: Describe secret cleanup and rotation actions after live testing.

notes.note-1: Phase-9 must eliminate plaintext-oriented secret handling from the standard operator workflow.
notes.note-2: docs/OPERATORS.md now defines approved secret source types, disallowed secret source types, canonical env-file locations, and mandatory rotation follow-up.

contract.purpose: Define a repeatable deployment and update workflow for server and client hosts instead of ad hoc manual staging.

export-deployServer.PURPOSE: Stage or update the server role with the canonical layout and bounded evidence.

export-deployClient.PURPOSE: Stage or update the client role with the canonical layout and bounded evidence.

notes.note-1: Repeatable deployment must preserve artifact identity and emit enough evidence to diagnose partial rollout failures.
notes.note-2: scripts/deploy-live.sh now provides a governed dry-run plus role-aware canonical staging flow for server and client hosts.

contract.purpose: Define managed systemd services for n0wss server and client roles with explicit start, stop, restart, and evidence behavior.

export-defineServerUnit.PURPOSE: Pin the server service ExecStart, restart policy, and environment sources.

export-defineClientUnit.PURPOSE: Pin the client service ExecStart, restart policy, and environment sources.

notes.note-1: Service-managed startup must remain traceable through the same CliApp and SessionManager anchors as manual live waves.
notes.note-2: Managed server and client unit files now pin EnvironmentFile paths under /opt/n0wss/env, canonical WorkingDirectory under /opt/n0wss, and bounded log targets under /var/log.

contract.purpose: Define bounded retention and rotation for n0wss logs so operators retain useful evidence without unbounded log growth.

export-defineRotationPolicy.PURPOSE: Pin retention count, file size policy, and post-rotate behavior for n0wss logs.

notes.note-1: Rotation policy must preserve the current-wave evidence packet until the operator explicitly closes it.
notes.note-2: deploy/logrotate/n0wss now rotates server, client, and bad-auth logs daily with maxsize 5M, rotate 7, compression, and copytruncate continuity.

contract.purpose: Define the stable operator runbook for install, start, stop, update, rollback, restart evidence, and bounded failure triage.

export-documentInstall.PURPOSE: Describe the canonical install and first-start procedure.

export-documentRecovery.PURPOSE: Describe restart, rollback, and evidence-capture actions.

notes.note-1: The runbook must describe only proven paths and must reference the same evidence commands used by the verification plan.
notes.note-2: docs/OPERATORS.md now documents managed install or update, systemctl lifecycle, journalctl evidence capture, and bounded rollback using the governed Phase-9 artifacts.

contract.purpose: Define the second live verification wave around service-managed startup, restart, reconnect, repeated smoke requests, and bounded evidence capture.

export-runManagedSmoke.PURPOSE: Execute repeated service-managed smoke requests after systemd launch.

export-runRestartRecovery.PURPOSE: Exercise controlled restart and reconnect behavior under managed services.

notes.note-1: Phase-9 converts the first green live wave into a repeatable service-managed deployment and recovery path.
notes.note-2: On March 28, 2026 the managed wave passed on ghost-srv and ghost-cli: repeated smoke requests returned Example Domain through systemd-managed services, restart recovery preserved readiness, and the bad-auth path rejected with redacted credential evidence.

M-TEST-GOVERNANCE-SYNC.NAME: TestGovernanceSync

export-syncTestHeaders.PURPOSE: Commit the prepared MODULE_CONTRACT, MODULE_MAP, and CHANGE_SUMMARY headers for substantive test files.

notes.note-1: The prepared test markup already exists locally and must be formalized before any new release tag is cut.

export-writeReleaseEntry.PURPOSE: Add the v0.3.0 release notes entry with managed deployment and live-wave evidence.

contract.purpose: Close the v0.3.0 release gate with a clean governed tree, synchronized artifacts, green release checks, and explicit tag readiness.

export-runReleaseGate.PURPOSE: Execute the release-prep checks and summarize tag readiness for v0.3.0.

notes.note-1: On March 28, 2026 the local release gate ran green: cargo clippy passed, cargo test passed with 88 unit tests plus 1 release smoke, git status was clean, git diff --check was clean, and the remote tag baseline remained v0.2.0.

contract.purpose: Align the existing tag-driven GitHub source release workflow with the v0.3.0 changelog and release gate outcome.

export-runTelegramSmoke.PURPOSE: Execute the Telegram-specific connect and reconnect checks through the governed SOCKS5 endpoint.

notes.note-1: On 2026-03-28 Telegram Desktop was exercised through a governed local SOCKS5 path by forwarding 127.0.0.1:1080 to the managed client host listener and switching the local system SOCKS proxy to the forwarded endpoint.
notes.note-2: The initial connect packet at 17:18:16Z through 17:18:26Z produced BLOCK_PARSE_SOCKS5_REQUEST, BLOCK_SELECT_TRANSPORT, and BLOCK_PUMP_BIDIRECTIONAL markers on Telegram target IPs under 149.154.0.0/16 and 91.108.0.0/16.
notes.note-3: The reconnect packet at 17:19:14Z through 17:19:32Z produced a fresh Telegram-target request burst with the same anchored transport-selection and bridge-pump trajectory plus accepted WSS handshakes on the server host.

contract.purpose: Define the exact moment and exact parameters for handing the final Telegram Desktop test to the operator.

export-prepareFinalTelegramHandoff.PURPOSE: Prepare the exact SSH forward and SOCKS5 settings the operator must use for the final Telegram Desktop acceptance test.

contract.purpose: Run the final manual Telegram Desktop acceptance wave while bounded evidence is captured on both fresh hosts.

export-runFinalTelegramAcceptance.PURPOSE: Coordinate the operator-led Telegram Desktop wave with bounded evidence capture on both hosts.

notes.note-1: On 2026-03-29 the rebuilt-host acceptance wave reached a green basic Telegram Desktop path through the governed local SSH forward to the managed client listener.
notes.note-2: The operator confirmed successful connection and text-message delivery without observed connection instability during the bounded acceptance window; media-heavy results remain separated under M-FINAL-VOICE-AND-MEDIA.

contract.purpose: Separate media-heavy Telegram acceptance scenarios such as photos, large files, and calls from the basic connect path.

export-runMediaAcceptance.PURPOSE: Record photo, large-file, and call-path acceptance results as separable evidence packets.

notes.note-1: On 2026-03-29 the operator reported green photo, media, and multi-gigabyte file transfer through the rebuilt-host Telegram Desktop path, with upload behavior described as fast and stable.
notes.note-2: Voice and video calls reached ringing and answer state but stalled at Telegram key-exchange, while the basic connect path remained healthy; this was classified as a media-specific call-path gap rather than a collapse of the verified TCP or SOCKS5 envelope.

contract.purpose: Assemble a bounded final acceptance packet from both hosts and the operator Telegram Desktop session without requiring blind reruns.

export-assembleFinalEvidence.PURPOSE: Assemble success or failure packets for the final acceptance wave with first-divergent-block discipline.

notes.note-1: The bounded final packet now contains separate readiness, basic-acceptance, media-success, and call-failure sections for the rebuilt-host Telegram Desktop wave.
notes.note-2: The failure summary preserves the observed call symptom, keeps the verified message and file path green, and points the next action at a later UDP-capable calls phase instead of replaying the same TCP or SOCKS5 acceptance steps.

contract.purpose: Produce the final go or no-go decision for the Telegram Desktop use case on the rebuilt two-host environment.

export-decideFinalAcceptance.PURPOSE: Summarize the final acceptance evidence into a go/no-go decision for the operator.

notes.note-1: Bounded decision on 2026-03-29: go for Telegram Desktop text messages, photo send, ordinary media, and large-file transfer on the rebuilt two-host environment through the governed SSH-forwarded SOCKS5 path.
notes.note-2: Bounded decision on 2026-03-29: no-go for Telegram Desktop use cases that require voice or video calls until a later UDP-capable calls phase exists.

export-pushReleasedBaselineToMaster.PURPOSE: Push the released v0.3.2 commit to origin/master.

contract.purpose: Define the transport-agnostic datagram envelope, association identity, size bounds, and error taxonomy for UDP-capable proxy flows.

export-datagramEnvelope.PURPOSE: Represent one normalized datagram plus addressing and association metadata.

export-datagramAssociationId.PURPOSE: Represent one stable UDP association identity.

contract.purpose: Add governed SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE ingress, relay bind handling, and UDP packet normalization without breaking the existing CONNECT path.

export-handleUdpAssociate.PURPOSE: Negotiate SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE and return the governed relay bind.

export-parseUdpDatagram.PURPOSE: Normalize one SOCKS5 UDP relay packet into the shared datagram contract.

contract.purpose: Track active UDP associations, governed relay endpoints, idle timers, and deterministic cleanup for datagram traffic.

export-openAssociation.PURPOSE: Register one active UDP association and reserve relay state.

export-closeAssociation.PURPOSE: Close one UDP association and free relay state deterministically.

contract.purpose: Coordinate UDP association lifecycle, outbound or inbound datagram dispatch, and session-side cleanup rules.

export-forwardOutboundDatagram.PURPOSE: Forward one normalized outbound datagram into the selected transport path.

contract.purpose: Encapsulate governed datagrams inside WSS frames and recover them on the opposite side without altering the existing stream tunnel path.

export-sendDatagram.PURPOSE: Send one governed datagram over the WSS control path.

export-receiveDatagram.PURPOSE: Decode one inbound WSS datagram frame back into the shared contract.

contract.purpose: Open real UDP sockets on the server side, relay outbound datagrams to remote targets, and return inbound packets to the owning association.

export-relayOutboundDatagram.PURPOSE: Relay one outbound datagram to the remote UDP target.

export-relayInboundDatagram.PURPOSE: Return one inbound datagram to the owning tunnel association.

contract.purpose: Select the governed datagram transport path, initially with WSS-backed datagrams and an explicit extension point for later parity work.

export-selectTransport.PURPOSE: Resolve one datagram transport path for a governed UDP association.

contract.purpose: Describe the bounded support claim for Telegram Desktop calls over the governed UDP-capable path without implying universal unblock guarantees.

export-documentTelegramCallsProfile.PURPOSE: Document the bounded Telegram Desktop calls support envelope.

contract.purpose: Run a dedicated Telegram voice or video calls verification wave over the new UDP-capable path.

export-runTelegramCallsWave.PURPOSE: Execute one governed Telegram calls verification wave.

contract.purpose: Define bounded evidence packets for Telegram call setup, media flow, reconnect, and first-divergent-block failure triage.

export-captureTelegramCallsEvidence.PURPOSE: Capture bounded evidence packets for Telegram calls verification.

M-CALLS-DEPLOY-BOOTSTRAP.NAME: CallsDeployBootstrap

export-prepareCallsHandoff.PURPOSE: Prepare the exact operator instructions for the live Telegram Desktop calls wave.

export-runCallsVideoWave.PURPOSE: Coordinate the operator video-call attempt and bounded UDP evidence capture.

export-runCallsReconnectWave.PURPOSE: Run one fresh reconnect call attempt and capture association-lifecycle evidence.

export-captureCallsEvidencePacket.PURPOSE: Capture the bounded packet set for the live Telegram calls wave.

export-issueCallsDecision.PURPOSE: Issue the final bounded calls support decision from the live evidence packets.

DF-CLIENT-BOOT.NAME: Client bootstrap
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.TRIGGER: The operator starts n0wss in client mode on a local machine.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.step-1: CliApp parses startup arguments and selects client mode.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.step-2: Config loads validated proxy bind settings, remote endpoints, auth material, transport limits, timeout policy, retry settings, and burst thresholds.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.step-3: Observability installs tracing, counters, gauges, histograms, sliding-window burst detection, and peak-rate reset timers.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.step-4: SessionManager initializes registry, transport selector, typed effect handler, and cancellation-token hierarchy.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.step-5: Socks5Proxy binds the local listener while WssGateway prepares the remote control surface.
DF-CLIENT-BOOT.evidence: Startup logs, config validation marker, observability init marker, metrics registration, and successful listener bind evidence.

DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.NAME: SOCKS5 request normalization
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.TRIGGER: A local proxy-aware application sends a SOCKS5 CONNECT request.
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.step-1: Socks5Proxy accepts the local connection and parses SOCKS5 negotiation.
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.step-2: Socks5Proxy parses the CONNECT request into a normalized ProxyIntent.
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.step-3: Socks5Proxy attempts try_send into a bounded channel consumed by ProxyBridge.
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.step-4: If the queue is full or closed, Socks5Proxy maps the failure to an immediate client-visible reply code and closes the connection.
DF-SOCKS5-INTENT.evidence: Socks5 parse marker, queue submission marker, fast failure timing checks, and deterministic failures for malformed requests.

DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.NAME: Transport resolution and fallback
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.TRIGGER: ProxyBridge needs a transport for a target address.
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.step-1: ProxyBridge requests SessionManager.resolveStream for the target described by ProxyIntent.
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.step-2: SessionManager delegates to TransportSelector which tries IrohAdapter.openStream with a bounded timeout and adapter cancel contract.
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.step-3: If iroh fails or times out, TransportSelector attempts WssGateway.openStream sequentially.
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.step-4: TransportSelector inspects adapter-scoped TaskTracker state during cleanup-sensitive tests and returns one ResolvedStream or a combined error describing both attempts plus any contract violation.
DF-TRANSPORT-FALLBACK.evidence: Transport selection marker, adapter contract marker, fallback marker, and exactly one active transport owner.

DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.NAME: SOCKS5 request tunnel
DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.step-1: ProxyBridge receives ProxyIntent from the bounded queue.
DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.step-2: ProxyBridge asks SessionManager for a ResolvedStream.
DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.step-3: ProxyBridge pumps bytes between the local client stream and the resolved transport stream.
DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.step-4: ProxyBridge notifies SessionManager when the stream completes or fails.
DF-SOCKS5-REQUEST.evidence: Queue consumption marker, transport selection marker, pump marker, and end-to-end proxy success test.

DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.NAME: Session effect dispatch
DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.TRIGGER: SessionManager receives a session lifecycle event.
DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.step-1: SessionManager passes the event into the pure StateMachine.transition function.
DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.step-2: StateMachine returns the new SessionState and an ordered list of top-level SessionEffect values.
DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.step-3: EffectHandler.applyAll forwards each effect to the target component without branching on business logic.
DF-SESSION-EFFECTS.evidence: Session effect marker, state transition tests, and effect-handler branch-count assertions.

DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.NAME: Branch baseline comparison
DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.TRIGGER: The operator wants GitHub default-branch state to match the already released stable baseline.
DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.step-1: The controller compares local HEAD, v0.3.2, and origin/master.
DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.step-2: The controller confirms the stable sync target is the released baseline commit rather than an unreviewed newer snapshot.
DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.step-3: The controller confirms origin/master can be fast-forward synchronized without rewriting history.
DF-BRANCH-BASELINE-CHECK.evidence: Git commit identities, tag identities, ahead or behind state, and a bounded branch-sync target.

DF-BRANCH-TRUTH-SYNC.NAME: Branch sync truth alignment
DF-BRANCH-TRUTH-SYNC.TRIGGER: Release-facing branch sync documentation is prepared before pushing master.
DF-BRANCH-TRUTH-SYNC.step-1: README and operator docs state that the branch push is a visibility sync for GitHub UI.
DF-BRANCH-TRUTH-SYNC.step-2: The wording preserves the already verified Telegram Desktop baseline and the explicit call-support gap.
DF-BRANCH-TRUTH-SYNC.evidence: No release drift between visible branch messaging and the v0.3.2 baseline truth.

DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.NAME: GitHub master synchronization
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.TRIGGER: The branch sync gate passes and the operator approves publication of the visible repository tree.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.step-1: The controller confirms the tree is clean and still points at the approved stable baseline.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.step-2: The controller pushes local master to origin/master.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.step-3: GitHub default-branch file view now exposes the same state that was already tagged as v0.3.2.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC.evidence: Successful git push output and remote branch identity matching the approved local HEAD.

DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.NAME: Released master target freeze
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.TRIGGER: A corrective branch sync is needed because local HEAD has moved beyond the released baseline.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.step-1: The controller resolves the commit behind v0.3.2.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.step-2: The controller explicitly excludes newer local commits from the publish target.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.step-3: The released commit becomes the only allowed sync destination for origin/master.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TARGET.evidence: Resolved tag commit identity, comparison with HEAD, and a frozen publish target record.

DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TRUTH.NAME: Released master messaging sync
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TRUTH.TRIGGER: Docs are aligned before pushing origin/master to the released commit.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TRUTH.step-1: README and operator docs explain that tag v0.3.2 already exists.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TRUTH.step-2: They explain that master sync targets the released snapshot only, not the newer local planning state.
DF-RELEASED-MASTER-TRUTH.evidence: Branch-sync wording stays bounded to the released baseline publication step.

DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC-032.NAME: GitHub master synchronization to released baseline
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC-032.TRIGGER: The released-master gate passes for v0.3.2.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC-032.step-1: The controller confirms clean-tree and the frozen released target.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC-032.step-3: GitHub default-branch browsing now matches the released stable baseline commit rather than the old branch tip.
DF-GITHUB-MASTER-SYNC-032.evidence: Successful push output and post-push remote branch identity equal to v0.3.2.

DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.NAME: SOCKS5 UDP associate negotiation
DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.TRIGGER: A proxy-aware client such as Telegram requests UDP relay support over SOCKS5.
DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.step-1: The client opens a SOCKS5 control channel and requests UDP ASSOCIATE.
DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.step-2: Socks5UdpAssociate allocates a governed local UDP relay bind and returns the relay address to the client.
DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.step-3: Subsequent UDP packets are normalized into the shared datagram contract and tied to the created association.
DF-SOCKS5-UDP-ASSOCIATE.evidence: UDP ASSOCIATE marker, governed relay bind proof, and normalized datagram parsing evidence.

DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.NAME: Outbound datagram relay
DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.TRIGGER: A Telegram media datagram arrives at the local UDP relay endpoint.
DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.step-1: Socks5UdpAssociate normalizes the UDP relay packet into a datagram envelope.
DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.step-2: DatagramSessionManager resolves the owning association and forwards the datagram through the selected transport path.
DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.step-3: WssDatagramGateway carries the datagram to the remote UdpEgressRelay, which forwards it to the target UDP endpoint.
DF-UDP-OUTBOUND.evidence: UDP ASSOCIATE marker, datagram session marker, WSS datagram marker, and outbound relay marker.

DF-UDP-INBOUND.NAME: Inbound datagram return path
DF-UDP-INBOUND.TRIGGER: A remote UDP endpoint returns media traffic to an active association.
DF-UDP-INBOUND.step-1: UdpEgressRelay receives the remote UDP packet and maps it back to the owning association.
DF-UDP-INBOUND.step-2: WssDatagramGateway encapsulates the inbound datagram and returns it to the client side.
DF-UDP-INBOUND.step-3: DatagramSessionManager and Socks5UdpAssociate emit the packet through the governed local UDP relay socket to the application.
DF-UDP-INBOUND.evidence: Inbound relay marker, WSS datagram receive marker, and local UDP relay egress proof.

DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.NAME: UDP association lifecycle
DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.TRIGGER: A UDP association is created, idles, reconnects, or is torn down.
DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.step-1: UdpAssociationRegistry records the active association and its relay metadata.
DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.step-2: DatagramSessionManager updates activity timestamps and enforces bounded idle cleanup.
DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.step-3: Association shutdown closes local relay state and remote relay ownership deterministically.
DF-UDP-ASSOCIATION-LIFECYCLE.evidence: Association-open marker, activity or timeout evidence, and deterministic cleanup proof.

DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.NAME: Telegram call signaling and media split
DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.TRIGGER: Telegram Desktop starts a voice or video call through n0wss.
DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.step-1: The existing TCP SOCKS5 path continues to carry Telegram signaling and setup traffic.
DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.step-2: The call media path switches to SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE and governed datagram relay.
DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.step-3: Success requires both signaling evidence and datagram media evidence; failure packets must classify the first divergent layer.
DF-TELEGRAM-CALL-PATH.evidence: Separated TCP signaling markers, UDP association markers, media relay markers, and bounded call-failure packets.

DF-REPLY-MAPPING.NAME: SOCKS5 reply mapping
DF-REPLY-MAPPING.TRIGGER: A proxy request fails before or after pump startup.
DF-REPLY-MAPPING.step-1: Socks5Proxy or ProxyBridge classifies ProxyError as infrastructure, policy, target, or post-pump failure.
DF-REPLY-MAPPING.step-2: Pre-pump failures map to an exact SOCKS5 reply code through exhaustive enum matching without wildcard arms.
DF-REPLY-MAPPING.step-3: Post-pump failures produce no new reply and close the transport path cleanly.
DF-REPLY-MAPPING.evidence: Reply mapping marker, exhaustive variant tests, and exact reply-code assertions.

DF-BACKPRESSURE.NAME: Bounded queue and session limit pressure
DF-BACKPRESSURE.TRIGGER: Incoming ProxyIntent load exceeds queue or session capacity.
DF-BACKPRESSURE.step-1: Socks5Proxy attempts to enqueue ProxyIntent using try_send.
DF-BACKPRESSURE.step-2: If the queue is full, Socks5Proxy sends a fast SOCKS5 failure reply and records queue-rejection metrics.
DF-BACKPRESSURE.step-3: If SessionRegistry is at capacity, ProxyBridge converts the limit error into a fast client-visible reply and records session-limit metrics.
DF-BACKPRESSURE.step-4: BurstDetector records rejection timestamps in a sliding window, updates peak-rate gauges, and rate-limits alert logs.
DF-BACKPRESSURE.evidence: Queue saturation marker, session limit marker, metrics assertions, sliding-window peak-rate metrics, and response-time checks below the configured safety budget.

DF-SHUTDOWN.NAME: Controlled shutdown
DF-SHUTDOWN.TRIGGER: The operator or runtime sends a stop signal while sessions are active.
DF-SHUTDOWN.step-1: CliApp cancels accept_token and stops new accepts in Socks5Proxy and WssGateway.
DF-SHUTDOWN.step-2: CliApp waits for the configured drain timeout and then cancels bridge_token.
DF-SHUTDOWN.step-3: ProxyBridge drains active pumps while SessionState transitions into Closing and Closed states.
DF-SHUTDOWN.step-4: CliApp cancels transport_token and SessionManager drains the registry while adapters close their TaskTracker scopes and release resources.
DF-SHUTDOWN.step-5: The process exits only after sessions are Closed or forced timeout policy triggers.
DF-SHUTDOWN.evidence: Ordered shutdown markers proving no leaked sessions or hanging streams remain.

DF-RELEASE-README.NAME: Release quickstart path
DF-RELEASE-README.TRIGGER: A GitHub visitor opens the repository before testing the project.
DF-RELEASE-README.step-1: The user reads README overview and requirements.
DF-RELEASE-README.step-2: The user follows the documented quickstart commands and config examples.
DF-RELEASE-README.step-3: The documented commands align with the current CLI and verification surfaces.
DF-RELEASE-README.evidence: README command review, quickstart alignment, and no stale paths or flags.

DF-GITHUB-CI.NAME: GitHub verification path
DF-GITHUB-CI.TRIGGER: A maintainer pushes a branch or opens a pull request before release.
DF-GITHUB-CI.step-1: GitHub Actions checks out the repository.
DF-GITHUB-CI.step-2: CI runs the repository lint and test commands that match local release gates.
DF-GITHUB-CI.step-3: A green workflow becomes the precondition for tagging a release.
DF-GITHUB-CI.evidence: Workflow command alignment and green CI status.

DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.NAME: Local release smoke
DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.TRIGGER: A maintainer prepares the first GitHub release candidate.
DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.step-1: The maintainer starts the runtime using documented commands.
DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.step-2: A single SOCKS5 request crosses the implemented bridge path.
DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.step-3: The maintainer shuts the process down and confirms clean drain behavior.
DF-LOCAL-SMOKE.evidence: Startup, reply mapping, bridge pump, and shutdown anchors all appear in one narrow smoke run.

DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.NAME: Two-host live deployment
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.TRIGGER: The operator prepares the first live wave on two clean remote hosts.
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.step-1: The operator builds the release binary and pins the deployed artifact identity.
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.step-2: The operator stages the binary, config, and TLS assets on the server host.
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.step-3: The operator stages the binary and client config on the client host.
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.step-4: The operator verifies executable paths, log paths, and expected listener ports on both hosts.
DF-LIVE-DEPLOY.evidence: Matching binary identity, fixed host roles, and no repository artifact containing raw credentials.

DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.NAME: Live server bootstrap
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.TRIGGER: The operator starts n0wss in server mode on the remote WSS host.
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.step-1: CliApp starts in server mode and loads validated config.
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.step-2: TlsContext loads the pinned live certificate and key.
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.step-3: Observability initializes and WssGateway binds the live listener.
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.step-4: The host records startup anchors, process identity, and bound socket state.
DF-LIVE-SERVER-BOOT.evidence: Config, TLS, and startup anchors plus host-local bound-listener proof.

DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.NAME: Live client bootstrap
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.TRIGGER: The operator starts n0wss in client mode on the remote client host.
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.step-1: CliApp starts in client mode and loads validated config for the remote WSS endpoint.
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.step-2: Observability initializes and SessionManager prepares transport selection.
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.step-3: Socks5Proxy binds the local client-facing ingress port.
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.step-4: The host records startup anchors, process identity, and bound SOCKS5 socket state.
DF-LIVE-CLIENT-BOOT.evidence: Config and startup anchors plus host-local SOCKS5 listener proof.

DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.NAME: Live two-host smoke
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.TRIGGER: A SOCKS5-aware request is sent on the live client host after both sides are ready.
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.step-1: The client host sends one deterministic SOCKS5 request through the local ingress port.
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.step-2: SessionManager resolves transport and the live tunnel reaches the server host.
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.step-3: WssGateway and AuthPolicy validate the live remote session and ProxyBridge pumps the stream.
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.step-4: The response returns to the client host and both hosts capture narrow evidence artifacts.
DF-LIVE-E2E-SMOKE.evidence: Transport-selection, reply-mapping, and pump anchors plus deterministic smoke output metadata.

DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.NAME: Live auth rejection
DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.TRIGGER: The operator intentionally launches a bad-auth live attempt after the success path.
DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.step-1: The client host retries with invalid auth material.
DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.step-2: The server host reaches AuthPolicy validation and rejects the handshake.
DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.step-3: No active session is created and the client sees a deterministic failure.
DF-LIVE-AUTH-REJECT.evidence: Auth validation anchor, redacted failure evidence, and no surviving session proof.

DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.NAME: Live controlled shutdown
DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.TRIGGER: The operator stops both live processes after the smoke paths complete.
DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.step-1: The operator stops new accepts on the client and server sides.
DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.step-2: Active sessions and proxy pumps drain according to the existing shutdown contract.
DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.step-3: The operator captures final log tails and socket state to prove no hung listeners remain.
DF-LIVE-SHUTDOWN.evidence: Shutdown anchors, empty listener state, and bounded final evidence artifacts from both hosts.

DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.NAME: Managed deployment packaging
DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.TRIGGER: The operator prepares a repeatable deployment or update for managed hosts.
DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.step-1: The operator builds or selects one governed release artifact.
DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.step-2: DeployScripting stages the artifact into the canonical DeployLayout on the target host.
DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.step-3: The deploy workflow emits bounded evidence for artifact identity, switched paths, and service inputs.
DF-DEPLOY-PACKAGE.evidence: Release checksum, target path proof, and bounded deploy output without raw secrets.

DF-SERVICE-BOOT.NAME: Managed service startup
DF-SERVICE-BOOT.TRIGGER: Systemd starts or restarts an n0wss unit on a managed host.
DF-SERVICE-BOOT.step-1: Systemd resolves the environment file, working directory, and ExecStart command for the selected role.
DF-SERVICE-BOOT.step-2: CliApp starts the requested runtime mode and emits startup anchors.
DF-SERVICE-BOOT.step-3: The operator captures service state, socket state, and bounded logs to prove readiness.
DF-SERVICE-BOOT.evidence: systemctl active state, MainPID, startup anchors, and bound socket proof.

DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.NAME: Managed service update
DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.TRIGGER: The operator rolls out a new binary or config under the managed deployment shape.
DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.step-1: DeployScripting copies the new artifact into a non-live path and validates identity.
DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.step-2: The active service is stopped or restarted through SystemdUnits under bounded operator control.
DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.step-3: The updated service emits a fresh readiness packet and leaves no stale listeners behind.
DF-SERVICE-UPDATE.evidence: Changed checksum, new MainPID, restart evidence, and no stale listener state.

DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.NAME: Secret material handling
DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.TRIGGER: The operator supplies runtime auth or host-side deployment secrets.
DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.step-1: SecretHygiene pins the accepted source for the secret material.
DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.step-2: DeployLayout and DeployScripting place the secret into the approved host-local location with restricted permissions.
DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.step-3: OperatorRunbook captures only redacted evidence that the secret source was consumed correctly.
DF-SECRET-MATERIAL.evidence: Restricted file mode or env-source proof, redacted logs, and no repository-stored raw secret values.

DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.NAME: Managed restart and reconnect wave
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.TRIGGER: The operator validates managed startup, repeated requests, restart recovery, and bad-auth behavior after Phase-9 deployment hardening.
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.step-1: The managed server and client services start through systemd using the canonical deploy layout.
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.step-2: The operator runs repeated deterministic smoke requests through the managed SOCKS5 endpoint.
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.step-3: The operator restarts one or both services and confirms reconnect plus readiness evidence.
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.step-4: The operator runs one bounded bad-auth check and captures a separate redacted evidence packet.
DF-LIVE-WAVE-2.evidence: Service readiness, repeated smoke success, restart-recovery evidence, reject-path evidence, and bounded logs.

DF-RELEASE-DIRTY-TREE-CLEANUP.NAME: Governed release tree cleanup
DF-RELEASE-DIRTY-TREE-CLEANUP.TRIGGER: The maintainer prepares the next release while governed test files still contain uncommitted GRACE markup updates.
DF-RELEASE-DIRTY-TREE-CLEANUP.step-1: TestGovernanceSync reviews the prepared test-file markup changes and ensures they stay inside the approved governed scope.
DF-RELEASE-DIRTY-TREE-CLEANUP.step-2: The maintainer commits the synchronized test governance changes so the release candidate no longer depends on a dirty worktree.
DF-RELEASE-DIRTY-TREE-CLEANUP.evidence: Clean git status for governed test files and committed GRACE markup headers.

DF-RELEASE-MESSAGE-SYNC.NAME: Release-facing message alignment
DF-RELEASE-MESSAGE-SYNC.TRIGGER: The repository reaches managed deployment readiness and release-facing copy must reflect the implemented posture.
DF-RELEASE-MESSAGE-SYNC.step-1: ReleaseMessaging removes stale wording about deployment still being unfinished.
DF-RELEASE-MESSAGE-SYNC.step-2: README and operator-facing release language describe the current managed deployment and source-first release shape consistently.
DF-RELEASE-MESSAGE-SYNC.evidence: README and operator docs align with the completed deployment and live-wave phases.

DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.NAME: Changelog and tag alignment
DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.TRIGGER: The next release candidate is prepared after remote tags already include v0.2.0.
DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.step-1: Changelog030 determines that the next release version is v0.3.0 from the remote tag baseline.
DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.step-2: The changelog entry lists the implemented managed deployment surface and the second managed live wave.
DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.step-3: The release notes align with the existing GitHub source-release workflow.
DF-CHANGELOG-TAG-ALIGNMENT.evidence: CHANGELOG.md contains a correct 0.3.0 entry and no release scope drift remains.

DF-RELEASE-GATE.NAME: Release gate closure
DF-RELEASE-GATE.TRIGGER: The maintainer asks whether the repository is ready to cut the v0.3.0 tag.
DF-RELEASE-GATE.step-1: The repository reaches a clean release-prep worktree for governed files.
DF-RELEASE-GATE.step-2: The maintainer runs clippy, cargo test, GRACE review, refresh, and status checks.
DF-RELEASE-GATE.step-3: The gate produces an explicit release-ready or blocked decision with evidence.
DF-RELEASE-GATE.evidence: Green release checks, synchronized GRACE artifacts, and a release-ready tag decision.

DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.NAME: GitHub source release v0.3.0
DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.TRIGGER: The release gate passes and the maintainer pushes the v0.3.0 tag.
DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.step-1: The maintainer creates the v0.3.0 git tag on the release-ready commit.
DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.step-2: GitHub Actions publishes the source-first release using the existing workflow.
DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.step-3: The published notes match the v0.3.0 changelog entry.
DF-GITHUB-SOURCE-RELEASE-0-3-0.evidence: Tag-triggered GitHub release execution and notes aligned with the prepared changelog.

DF-TELEGRAM-PROXY-CONFIG.NAME: Telegram SOCKS5 configuration
DF-TELEGRAM-PROXY-CONFIG.TRIGGER: An operator configures Telegram Desktop or Mobile to use the local n0wss endpoint as a SOCKS5 proxy.
DF-TELEGRAM-PROXY-CONFIG.step-1: The operator points Telegram at the local n0wss SOCKS5 listener address and port.
DF-TELEGRAM-PROXY-CONFIG.step-2: The configured client remains a standard SOCKS5 consumer and does not need a custom n0wss protocol.
DF-TELEGRAM-PROXY-CONFIG.evidence: Documented app-side settings aligned with the governed SOCKS5 client profile.

DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.NAME: Telegram connect path
DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.TRIGGER: Telegram attempts to establish connectivity through the local SOCKS5 proxy.
DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.step-1: Telegram issues SOCKS5 requests to the local n0wss client endpoint.
DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.step-2: Socks5Proxy parses the request, SessionManager resolves transport, and ProxyBridge pumps the stream through the remote WSS path.
DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.step-3: The response path returns to Telegram through the same proxied tunnel.
DF-TELEGRAM-CONNECT.evidence: SOCKS5 parse, transport selection, and pump anchors plus Telegram-visible connectivity proof.

DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.NAME: Telegram reconnect recovery
DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.TRIGGER: The n0wss client or managed service is restarted while Telegram remains configured for SOCKS5 usage.
DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.step-1: The managed n0wss service restarts and rebinds the local SOCKS5 listener.
DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.step-2: Telegram retries connection attempts through the same local proxy settings.
DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.step-3: The operator captures whether reconnect succeeds or fails deterministically.
DF-TELEGRAM-SESSION-RECOVERY.evidence: Managed readiness packet plus fresh SOCKS5 and pump markers after restart.

DF-TELEGRAM-EVIDENCE.NAME: Telegram failure and success evidence
DF-TELEGRAM-EVIDENCE.TRIGGER: Telegram-specific verification succeeds or diverges and needs a bounded packet for later triage.
DF-TELEGRAM-EVIDENCE.step-1: The operator captures Telegram app-side state, local SOCKS5 readiness, and n0wss anchored logs.
DF-TELEGRAM-EVIDENCE.step-2: The evidence packet separates app misconfiguration from tunnel-side divergence.
DF-TELEGRAM-EVIDENCE.evidence: Bounded Telegram-specific success or failure packets with first divergent block when applicable.

Phase-1.name: Foundation
Phase-1.goal: Define startup, config, logging, metrics, auth, TLS, limits, timeout, reply policy, retry, and burst primitives.
Phase-1.step-1: Initialize configuration module with transport, queue, limit, timeout, retry, and burst settings
Phase-1.step-2: Initialize structured observability, metrics, sliding-window burst detection, and peak-rate reset
Phase-1.step-4: Build TLS context loading and validation
Phase-1.step-5: Wire startup and graceful shutdown orchestration

Phase-2.name: Transport Boundaries
Phase-2.goal: Implement both transport boundaries behind shared stream, adapter, and task-tracking contracts.
Phase-2.step-1: Build WSS adapter, task tracker, and contract tests
Phase-2.step-2: Build iroh adapter, task tracker, and contract tests

Phase-3.name: Session Core
Phase-3.goal: Separate state transitions, effect routing, storage, and transport selection before building the orchestrator.
Phase-3.step-1: Implement pure session state machine and effect generation
Phase-3.step-2: Implement top-level typed SessionEffect contract and stable EffectHandler routing
Phase-3.step-3: Implement SessionRegistry with semaphore limits and drain support
Phase-3.step-4: Implement TransportSelector with sequential fallback, safety timeout, and contract-violation handling
Phase-3.step-5: Implement thin SessionManager orchestrator over registry, selector, state machine, effect handler, and local enum-hygiene tests
Phase-3.step-6: Wire startup and graceful shutdown orchestration

Phase-4.name: Proxy Ingress
Phase-4.goal: Accept and normalize local SOCKS5 requests into queueable ProxyIntent work items with fast failure semantics and accurate reply mapping.
Phase-4.step-1: Implement local SOCKS5 ingress listener, bounded intent queue, exact reply mapping without wildcard arms, and exhaustive category mapping

Phase-5.name: Bridge
Phase-5.goal: Process normalized proxy requests over transport-agnostic streams.
Phase-5.step-1: Implement queue-driven bridge workers, stream pumping, and pre-pump/post-pump error separation

Phase-6.name: Hardening
Phase-6.goal: Prove fallback, burst handling, timing, contract compliance, and shutdown behavior under non-ideal conditions.
Phase-6.step-1: Deepen effect-routing, fallback ownership, timeout, max-session tests, and local enum-hygiene checks
Phase-6.step-2: Deepen bounded queue saturation timing, reply semantics, exhaustive error mapping tests, and CI lint guards
Phase-6.step-3: Stress backpressure, total timeout, and controlled drain behavior
Phase-6.step-4: Validate burst-window accuracy, peak-rate metrics, alert-friendly observability, and deployment tuning guidance

Phase-7.name: Release Readiness
Phase-7.goal: Close the remaining markup debt, align release docs and GitHub workflows, and add a narrow smoke gate before external testing.
Phase-7.step-1: Align the governed crate-root export surface with the implemented module set
Phase-7.step-2: Prepare README, changelog, and operator-facing release documentation
Phase-7.step-3: Align GitHub CI and release workflow expectations with the repository state
Phase-7.step-4: Expose the deployable n0wss binary target required by release packaging and live staging
Phase-7.step-5: Add a deterministic release smoke path for startup, one proxied request, and shutdown

Phase-8.name: First Live Wave
Phase-8.goal: Prepare and execute the first two-host live verification wave with one success path, one auth rejection, and clean shutdown evidence.
Phase-8.step-1: Freeze host roles, paths, ports, and operator-only access assumptions for the two remote machines
Phase-8.step-2: Pin the release artifact, staging shape, and runtime assets deployed to both hosts
Phase-8.step-3: Fix the real TLS trust path, certificate files, and endpoint identity for the live WSS server
Phase-8.step-4: Launch the remote server host and prove WSS readiness with startup and socket evidence
Phase-8.step-5: Launch the remote client host and prove SOCKS5 readiness with startup and socket evidence
Phase-8.step-6: Run one deterministic live SOCKS5 smoke request through the two-host tunnel
Phase-8.step-7: Collect narrow high-signal logs, socket state, and smoke output artifacts from both hosts
Phase-8.step-8: Convert any live failure into a GRACE failure packet before reruns

Phase-9.name: Deployment Hardening
Phase-9.goal: Convert the first green live wave into a repeatable service-managed deployment shape with safer secret handling, bounded logs, and a second managed verification wave.
Phase-9.step-1: Define the canonical host filesystem layout, path ownership, and runtime-state boundaries for managed deployment
Phase-9.step-2: Replace plaintext-oriented operator secret handling with a governed file or env-based model and explicit rotation follow-up
Phase-9.step-3: Implement repeatable server and client deployment or update scripting around the canonical layout
Phase-9.step-4: Define managed server and client systemd services with explicit readiness and restart evidence
Phase-9.step-5: Add bounded log-retention policy that preserves current-wave evidence
Phase-9.step-6: Document install, start, stop, update, rollback, and evidence capture for the managed deployment shape
Phase-9.step-7: Execute the second live wave for service-managed startup, repeated smoke, restart recovery, and bad-auth verification

Phase-10.name: Release Preparation
Phase-10.goal: Prepare the repository for a coherent v0.3.0 source release by cleaning governed release blockers, synchronizing release-facing docs, and closing the release gate.
Phase-10.step-1: Commit the prepared GRACE markup in substantive test files so the governed release tree is clean
Phase-10.step-2: Align README and operator-facing release wording with the completed managed deployment surface
Phase-10.step-3: Add the v0.3.0 changelog entry for the managed deployment and live-wave scope
Phase-10.step-4: Run the release gate checks and record whether v0.3.0 is ready to tag
Phase-10.step-5: Prepare the tag-driven GitHub source release path for v0.3.0

Phase-11.name: Telegram Verification Wave
Phase-11.goal: Prove the existing SOCKS5-over-tunnel design works for Telegram as a proxy-aware client, while explicitly bounding unsupported unblock claims.
Phase-11.step-1: Define the supported Telegram-facing SOCKS5 profile and its explicit non-goals
Phase-11.step-2: Document and verify the readiness path for launching n0wss as a Telegram-facing SOCKS5 proxy
Phase-11.step-3: Execute a Telegram-specific connect and reconnect verification wave through the governed SOCKS5 endpoint
Phase-11.step-4: Capture bounded Telegram-specific success and failure evidence packets
Phase-11.step-5: Document the verified Telegram compatibility bounds without overstating unblock guarantees

Phase-12.name: Telegram Forward Clarification
Phase-12.goal: Repair the operator and verification contract so the proven local Telegram Desktop path explicitly includes the required SSH forward to the remote managed n0wss client.
Phase-12.step-1: Document the local-workstation Telegram Desktop path and the SSH local-forward prerequisite explicitly in the operator runbook
Phase-12.step-2: Align LV-007 and Telegram evidence requirements with the forwarded Desktop verification path
Phase-12.step-3: Synchronize the knowledge graph with the clarified Telegram Desktop operator path

Phase-13.name: Patch Release 0.3.1
Phase-13.goal: Prepare, gate, and publish the v0.3.1 patch release for the Telegram forward clarification wave after the existing v0.3.0 release baseline.
Phase-13.step-1: Add the v0.3.1 changelog entry for the Telegram forward clarification patch scope
Phase-13.step-2: Align release-facing docs with the verified Telegram Desktop forwarded path
Phase-13.step-3: Run the release gate and confirm v0.3.1 is ready to tag
Phase-13.step-4: Create and push the v0.3.1 tag through the existing GitHub source-release workflow

Phase-14.name: Final Telegram Acceptance On Fresh Hosts
Phase-14.goal: Rebaseline two fresh Ubuntu hosts, redeploy n0wss, hand off a verified Telegram Desktop path to the operator, and capture final acceptance evidence for real Telegram use.
Phase-14.step-1: Confirm both rebuilt Ubuntu hosts are reachable, clean, and ready for controlled deployment
Phase-14.step-2: Redeploy the governed managed n0wss stack onto both fresh hosts
Phase-14.step-3: Prove fresh service readiness before the operator Telegram Desktop test begins
Phase-14.step-4: Prepare the exact SSH forward and SOCKS5 settings for the operator handoff
Phase-14.step-5: Run the final Telegram Desktop acceptance wave with bounded evidence capture
Phase-14.step-6: Capture photo, file, and call-path acceptance results as separable media evidence
Phase-14.step-7: Assemble the bounded final evidence packet from both hosts and the operator session
Phase-14.step-8: Produce the final go/no-go decision for the Telegram Desktop use case on the rebuilt environment

Phase-15.name: Release Baseline 0.3.2
Phase-15.goal: Publish the current verified local baseline to GitHub as v0.3.2 before planning the UDP-capable Telegram calls architecture.
Phase-15.step-1: Add the bounded v0.3.2 changelog entry for the current verified baseline
Phase-15.step-2: Align release-facing docs with the verified Telegram Desktop envelope and current limitations
Phase-15.step-3: Synchronize GRACE artifacts with the v0.3.2 release truth
Phase-15.step-4: Run the release gate and confirm v0.3.2 is ready to tag
Phase-15.step-5: Create and push the v0.3.2 tag through the existing GitHub source-release workflow

Phase-16.name: GitHub Master Baseline Sync
Phase-16.goal: Fast-forward origin/master to the already released stable v0.3.2 baseline so GitHub default-branch browsing reflects the current verified repository state.
Phase-16.step-1: Confirm that the visible branch sync target is the current released baseline and not an unbounded newer snapshot
Phase-16.step-2: Align branch-sync wording so GitHub UI sync is not confused with a new runtime or release scope
Phase-16.step-3: Run the branch sync gate and confirm local master can safely fast-forward origin/master
Phase-16.step-4: Push the approved local master baseline to origin/master

Phase-17.name: Released Commit Master Sync
Phase-17.goal: Update origin/master to the already released v0.3.2 commit so GitHub default-branch browsing matches the stable baseline without publishing newer local planning commits.
Phase-17.step-1: Freeze the only allowed origin/master target as the released v0.3.2 commit
Phase-17.step-2: Align docs so this sync publishes the released snapshot only and excludes newer local planning commits
Phase-17.step-3: Run the gate for syncing origin/master specifically to v0.3.2

Phase-18.name: Telegram Calls Support
Phase-18.goal: Extend the governed proxy path with UDP-capable architecture so Telegram Desktop voice and video calls can be tested through SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE and bounded datagram relay.
Phase-18.step-1: Define the transport-agnostic datagram contract, association identity, and error taxonomy
Phase-18.step-2: Add governed SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE ingress and datagram normalization
Phase-18.step-3: Track active UDP associations, idle timers, and deterministic cleanup
Phase-18.step-4: Coordinate datagram association lifecycle and outbound or inbound dispatch
Phase-18.step-5: Carry governed datagrams over the existing WSS security boundary
Phase-18.step-6: Relay real UDP traffic on the server side and return inbound packets
Phase-18.step-7: Select the governed datagram transport path with explicit future extension points
Phase-18.step-8: Document the bounded Telegram Desktop calls support claim
Phase-18.step-9: Run a dedicated Telegram calls verification wave over the UDP-capable path
Phase-18.step-10: Capture bounded evidence packets for call setup, media flow, and failure triage

Phase-19.name: Telegram Calls Live Verification
Phase-19.goal: Deploy the UDP-capable n0wss baseline onto the fresh server pair and collect bounded live Telegram Desktop voice or video call evidence on the tested setup.
Phase-19.step-1: Capture fresh-host identity and baseline tooling evidence for the live calls wave
Phase-19.step-2: Deploy the UDP-capable n0wss build to the fresh client and server hosts
Phase-19.step-3: Prove client or server service readiness and UDP-capable runtime markers before operator handoff
Phase-19.step-4: Prepare the exact SSH-forward and SOCKS5 handoff for Telegram Desktop calls
Phase-19.step-5: Run one bounded Telegram voice-call live wave and capture separate signaling and media evidence
Phase-19.step-6: Run a separate Telegram video-call live wave without conflating it with the voice result
Phase-19.step-7: Trigger one fresh second call attempt and prove a new UDP association lifecycle
Phase-19.step-8: Assemble separate call-setup, media-flow, reconnect, and failure packets
Phase-19.step-9: Issue the bounded go-or-no-go decision for Telegram Desktop calls on the tested setup

ExecutionPolicy.worker-owns: Only module source files and module-local tests inside the approved write scope.
